MONOCULAR VIDEO → 4D HAND–OBJECT INTERACTION

Reconstructing a 4D Hand–Object Interaction from one RGB video

A nine-stage pipeline turns a single phone clip — a hand grasping a yogurt bottle and rotating it — into a metric 4D scene: an articulated hand mesh holding a textured 3D object that moves with the wrist. This page explains how each stage works and the specific controls used to make the reconstruction match the actual interaction in the video.

1. The input & the goal

Input: examples/grab.mp4 — a hand approaches a white/blue yogurt bottle, grasps it, and rotates the wrist. Goal: recover the same interaction in 3D over time (the hand pose, the object's shape & texture, and its 6D motion), tightly coupled as a physical grasp.

2. The pipeline (stages 0–8)

Each stage writes a self-contained bundle to disk, so stages are independently runnable, resumable and inspectable. Real backends are loaded lazily and only in --real mode.

0

Preprocess & Geometry

video → frames, metric depth + camera intrinsics

MoGe-2 (depth + intrinsics); identity extrinsics (static cam) or Depth-Anything-3 for real camera motion

1

Detect & Segment

per-frame hand boxes + object masks

WiLoR YOLO hand detector · SAM 2.1 video segmentation (point-prompted from the grasp)

2

Hand

per-frame MANO hand mesh (778 verts, 21 joints)

HaMeR → MANO, then re-anchored to metric depth

3

Object

textured object mesh + 6D pose track

SAM-3D-Objects (single-image → full textured 3D), scaled to metric, rotation coupled to the hand

4

Align

hand + object in one metric world frame

lift both into the shared camera/metric frame; record contact gap

5

Coarse Fit

temporally smoothed 4D motion

moving-average smoothing of hand verts/joints and object translation

6

Rectify

snap object to grasp + build contact correspondences

distance (<2cm) + surface-normal (<60°) gated hand→object anchors

7

Joint Grasp Optimization

close the hand on the object (final 4D HOI)

torch optimization of hand 6-DOF + object 6-DOF under contact / non-penetration / seat / smoothness energies

8

Evaluate

error-attribution report

active contacts, contact gap, penetration

Models behind the stages

ModelRoleStageWeights
MoGe-2metric depth + camera intrinsicsstage 0checkpoints/moge/
WiLoR (YOLO)hand detection boxesstage 1checkpoints/wilor/detector.pt
SAM 2.1object video segmentationstage 1checkpoints/sam2/
HaMeR + MANOper-frame 3D handstage 2checkpoints/hamer/, checkpoints/mano/
SAM-3D-Objectssingle-image textured 3D objectstage 3checkpoints/hf/ (separate env)

3. The shared idea: one metric frame

The whole reconstruction hangs on putting everything in the same metric camera frame. MoGe-2 gives per-pixel metric depth + intrinsics; that depth map is the common ruler. The hand, the object, and their contact are all expressed against it — which is exactly what the early bugs violated, and what the controls below restore.

4. Controls used to match the real interaction

Five targeted controls moved the reconstruction from "floating, untextured, fingertip-touch" to "metric, textured, wrist-rotating, firmly grasped". Each fixes a specific failure.

1 · Make it run at all

Problem

Scripts couldn't find checkpoints; the hand stage crashed loading MANO.

Root cause

Paths were CWD-relative; MANO lived in a nested folder; chumpy (needed to read MANO .pkl) won't import on numpy 2.

Control / fix

Anchored checkpoint paths to the repo root, resolved MANO from either layout, and restored the numpy scalar aliases before chumpy imports.

Result

Pipeline runs end-to-end on a single env.

2 · Ground the hand in metric depth

Problem

The hand floated metres away from the object — not holding anything.

Root cause

HaMeR places the hand with a fabricated focal length, so its absolute depth was 0–9.6 m and jittered wildly — a different frame than the metric object.

Control / fix

Kept HaMeR's metric hand shape, but re-anchored its position by back-projecting the hand region at the MoGe metric depth with the real intrinsics — the same frame the object lives in.

Result

Hand↔object distance: 6.6 m → 0.13 m.

3 · Reconstruct the real object

Problem

The object was a flat-coloured convex blob, nothing like the bottle.

Root cause

The model-free branch built a convex hull from one frame's depth, with no texture.

Control / fix

Wired SAM-3D-Objects: one masked frame → a full textured mesh (white body, blue cap, blueberry label). Scaled to the object's metric size measured from the mask + depth, decimated for speed, colours threaded to the viewer.

Result

Recognizable, textured bottle (4.5×11.7×4.5 cm).

4 · Make the object move correctly (6D)

Problem

The bottle translated but never rotated; in the video the wrist spins it.

Root cause

The object track was translation-only (rotation = identity).

Control / fix

A grasped object is rigid w.r.t. the hand, so I derive its rotation from the hand's rigid palm motion (Procrustes on wrist + finger-base joints), anchored at grasp onset.

Result

Bottle rotates with the wrist (up to ~180°).

5 · Close the hand into a real grasp

Problem

Only the fingertips touched the object; the grip looked loose.

Root cause

The contact model tied only fingertip vertices and optimized only the object — the hand was never closed. The 0.5 mm 'gap' metric measured just the nearest fingertip, hiding the looseness.

Control / fix

Added a joint hand+object optimization: per-frame hand 6-DOF + object 6-DOF under seat (surface→palm), proximity-recruited contact, non-penetration, temporal-smoothness and stay-near-HaMeR priors.

Result

Hand verts within 1 cm: 188 → 390 / 778; contacts 33k→58k.

5. Object: from blob to textured bottle

SAM-3D-Objects reconstructs a full textured mesh from a single masked frame. It is placed on the metric depth-lift trajectory and scaled to the object's real size (measured from the mask's pixel height at its measured depth), then its rotation is tied to the hand.

6. Object 6D: rotating with the wrist

Because the bottle is rigidly grasped, its rotation is inherited from the hand's rigid palm motion (Procrustes fit of wrist + finger-base joints, frame to frame, relative to grasp onset). Before grasp it stays at rest; once grasped it follows the wrist.

7. Grasp: joint hand + object optimization

The decisive control for grasp fidelity. Instead of only sliding the object to the fingertips, stage 7 jointly optimizes a per-frame hand rigid transform and the object 6D pose so the hand closes onto the object. The objective (torch + Adam):

E seatpull the object surface onto the palm (target = object cross-radius, so it is held, not buried) E contactproximity-recruited hand vertices onto the object surface — the contact set grows as the hand closes, giving envelopment rather than fingertips E penone-sided non-penetration (hand vertices must stay outside the object) E smoothtemporal smoothness of the hand & object motion E priorstay near the HaMeR hand and the stage-6 object placement (no unrealistic drift)
hand verts <1 cm of object: 188 → 390 / 778 active contacts: 33k → 58k contact gap: 0.5 mm

8. How "control" is exerted — the levers

"Making the reconstruction match the video" is, concretely, four kinds of control:

  • Metric grounding — anchor every component to MoGe metric depth so hand & object share one frame (kills the 6.6 m offset).
  • Evidence-faithful appearance — reconstruct the object's true shape + texture from the masked frame (SAM-3D), scaled to its measured metric size.
  • Physical coupling — a grasped object is rigid w.r.t. the hand, so its 6D motion is inherited from the wrist; the grasp is closed by joint optimization with non-penetration.
  • Regularization & priors — temporal smoothness and stay-near-perception priors keep the solution close to the observed evidence instead of drifting to a degenerate optimum.

9. Honest limitations

  • Rigid hand refinement. The grasp closes by moving the whole (already-curled) hand rigidly — finger articulation isn't re-posed, because MANO pose params aren't carried past stage 2. So envelopment plateaus (~390/778); true finger-closing needs differentiable MANO re-posing.
  • Residual penetration ~10 mm. The optimizer's penetration test uses a cheap nearest-vertex signed distance; an exact mesh SDF would reduce it but is slower / non-differentiable.
  • Absolute object orientation is a canonical "upright" choice from SAM-3D; the relative rotation tracks the wrist faithfully, but the starting tilt isn't pinned to the video frame.
  • Static camera assumption with MoGe (identity extrinsics); use --depth da3 for real camera motion.

10. Run & view

ActionCommand
Reconstructpython -m hoi_recon.cli --video examples/grab.mp4 --out runs/grab --real --hand hamer --object sam3d --depth moge
View 4D resulthoi_recon-view --run runs/grab
Re-run a subset... --stages 7-8 --force
Generated for the hoi_recon project · nine-stage monocular HOI reconstruction (MoGe · WiLoR · SAM 2 · HaMeR/MANO · SAM-3D-Objects) with metric grounding, hand-coupled object 6D, and joint grasp optimization.
